segments.s235-g49: چه (interrogative) + کردست (archaic/perfect 3rd sg) forms a question ‘what has he done’. با introduces the object ‘the king of Mazandaran’ (prepositional phrase: با + NP). The verb کردست (کرده‌است) is placed before the prepositional object in this poetic word order.
segments.s235-g50: ز (poetic preposition) means ‘from/by’; گرزش is the agent/tool phrase ‘his club’ or ‘a blow of his club’ and is the cause. چه آمد is an exclamatory/interrogative clause ‘what came/happened’, where آمد (past) is used in the sense ‘befell/happened to’. بران = بر + آن contracted, ‘upon those/that’, and مهتران is the plural noun ‘chiefs/elders’ (here ‘those chiefs’) placed last as the affected persons.
segments.s235-g51: گرانمایگان is a plural subject ‘the illustrious ones/nobles’. پاسخ is the direct object placed before the verb (typical Persian SOV). آراستند is past 3rd-person plural ‘they arranged/composed/prepared’ (آراستن), here meaning ‘composed a reply’.
segments.s235-g52: همه یکسر = ‘all at once’ (همه = everyone; یکسر with adverbial force ‘at one stroke/altogether’). از جای = ‘from their places’ (از + جای), and برخاستند is past 3rd-plural ‘they stood up/rose’ (برخاستن). The sequence gives the sudden collective motion.
segments.s235-g53: که‌گر is an archaic conditional particle meaning ‘if’. نامداران is the plural noun ‘the famed/renowned ones’. سقلاب and چین are place-names joined by و; together they specify which renowned men are meant. This entire phrase is the protasis (if-clause) subject.
segments.s235-g54: بایران is the prepositional phrase ‘to/into Iran’ formed by بـ (to/into) + ایران; here it marks the theatre of action. همی is the habitual/continuous particle (often found in epic Persian) giving iterative or emphatic colouring before the verbal phrase. رزم جستند is a verbal compound: رزم (battle) + جستند (pl. past of جستن) meaning ‘they strove for/sought battle’ i.e. ‘they fought’. و کین appends the purpose/quality ‘and (sought) vengeance’. The clause is past tense and hypothetical under که‌گر.
segments.s235-g55: نه ... شدست is negative perfect/ resultative: ‘not has become’. از لشکر ما کسی کم شدست literally ‘someone has become less from our army’ — i.e. ‘no one of our army has been lost’. کم functions as a predicate adjective ‘less/fewer’ and شدست is the archaic third-person perfect/ resultive ‘has become/has happened’. The negation nein (نه) scopes over the whole clause.
segments.s235-g56: The second coordinated negative clause mirrors the first: نه این کشور = ‘nor this country’. از خون دمادم شدست uses از خون ‘with/because of blood’ and دمادم (adverb/adjective) ‘continually/constantly’ to say the realm has not become drenched in blood. شدست again is the archaic perfect/resultive ‘has become’ in the negative construction.
segments.s235-g33: شکسته شدست is a compact past/resultative passive: شکسته (broken) + شدست (contracted form of شده‌است / شد است) yielding 'has been broken' or 'was broken'. آن سپاه گران is a noun phrase: آن (that/the) + سپاه (host/army) + گران (literally 'heavy' but here adjectival 'formidable/weighty') — adjective follows the noun as usual in Persian.
segments.s235-g34: چنان ساز functions as a brief descriptive clause: چنان (so/such) modifies ساز. ساز here is used poetically to mean 'arrangement/state' (not just a musical instrument). و آن لشکر بی‌کران is a coordinate noun phrase joined by و: آن (that) + لشکر (host/army) + بی‌کران (boundless/endless), placed after the first clause to amplify the scale.
segments.s235-g35: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' governing the following noun phrase. اندوه ('sorrow') is the abstract noun governed by ز. کاموس is a proper name (Kamus) coordinated with خاقان چین (the title 'Khagan of China'); خاقان is the title and چین is a genitive apposition specifying which khagan (i.e., 'of China').
segments.s235-g36: ببستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural transitive verb meaning 'they closed/tied/bound'. گفتی here is used as a noun 'talk/discourse' (object) rather than the 2nd-person verb; the following م + را together mark the first-person object (م ... را ≈ مرا 'me'). بر زمین is a locative phrase 'on/upon the ground'. The whole construction reads roughly as 'they bound/closed my speech (or bound me) to the ground.'
segments.s235-g37: سپاهی is a collective noun ('soldiers/army'); چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the following predicates. بسته و خسته are participial/adjectival complements ('enclosed/bound' and 'weary'), and شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was' that yields the resultative meaning 'was/became enclosed and exhausted.' Word order: subject — adverb — adjectives — auxiliary verb.
segments.s235-g38: دو بهره is a quantitative noun phrase 'two shares/parts/divisions'. ز گردنکشان is a partitive/ablative phrase 'from/of the گردنکشان' (here 'the rebels/proud ones'); بسته شد is passive/resultative 'were bound/secured/taken'. The sequence indicates that two portions were taken from/allocated among the rebels.
segments.s235-g39: بایران here functions as a destination ('to Iran') — in the manuscript it appears fused but syntactically equals به ایران. کشیدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they pulled/drew/carried'. بر پشت پیل is a locative phrase 'upon the elephant's back' (بر + پشت + پیل). The verb takes the implied direct object (them/it) carried on the elephant.
segments.s235-g40: زمین is the subject 'the ground/earth'. پر ز خون is a predicate phrase 'full of blood' (پر 'full' + ز 'of' + خون 'blood'). بود is the past copula 'was'. تا چند میل marks extent: تا ... میل = 'as far as a few/several miles'; چند is 'several', while میل here denotes a distance unit (archaic 'mile').
segments.s235-g1: پس ('then') is a temporal adverb starting the sentence. آگاهی ('news, information') is the subject that 'came' — آمد is the simple past of آمدن ('to come') and here means 'arrived' or 'was received'. بافراسیاب is a single orthographic unit referring to Afrasiab (the name appears here as the recipient/target of the arriving news).
segments.s235-g2: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). آتش ('fire') is the subject of the subordinate clause. برآمد is the past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), and دریای آب is a genitive phrase 'sea of water' (دریای = 'sea of', آب = 'water').
segments.s235-g3: ز here means 'from' or 'at the hands of' and introduces the agents causing the event. کاموس and منشور are personal names here (listed as agents), joined by و ('and'). خاقان is the title 'khaghan' (ruler) and چین specifies 'China' (i.e. the Chinese khaghan).
segments.s235-g4: شکستی ('a defeat') is the noun-subject; نو ('new') is its modifier. آمد is the past 'came' meaning 'befell/occurred'. بتوران is 'به توران' (to/onto Turan) indicating the locus affected, and زمین ('land') specifies 'the land/country' — together 'to the land of Turan'.
segments.s235-g5: از ایران ('from Iran') gives the origin. یکی لشکر literally 'one/ a single army' (یکی as numeral/indefinite), لشکر = 'army'. آمد بجنگ is the motion verb آمدن + بجنگ ('to fight' / 'for battle') expressing purpose: 'came to fight'.
segments.s235-g6: که here introduces the result/clause ('so that/so it was that'). شد is past 'became/was made'. چرخ گردنده is a compound 'the rotating/turning wheel' (چرخ often = fate or the celestial wheel; گردنده = 'turning/rotating'). را marks that phrase as definite/object; راه تنگ ('the way/path narrow') is the predicate complement meaning 'the way was made narrow' (i.e. the course of events was constrained).
segments.s235-g7: چهل روز ('forty days') gives duration (the word چهل is implicit in the listed words). یکسان همی جنگ بود: یکسان = 'unchanged/same', همی is an archaic/poetic continuous particle meaning 'still/ever', جنگ بود is 'there was war' or 'the fighting lasted' (بود is past of 'to be' used as main verb).
segments.s235-g8: شب و روز ('night and day') is a continuous-time adverbial. گیتی = 'the world'. بیک رنگ is an older compound meaning 'of one/uniform color' (بیک here functions as part of the adjective phrase), رنگ = 'color', and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause states that the world remained of one color day and night (i.e. there was no change).
segments.s235-g9: ز here introduces the cause or source ('because of' / 'from') and takes the following noun phrase. گرد is used as the noun 'dust' (not the verb) so ز + گرد سواران = 'because of the riders' dust'. نبود is the past negative of بود ('there was not') and follows the causal phrase; آفتاب (the thing absent) appears after the verb in this poetic word order: 'Because of the riders' dust, the sun was not.'
segments.s235-g10: چو functions temporally as 'when'. بیدار modifies بخت ('bakt' = Fortune) to give 'Fortune awake' (a nominal phrase). اندر آمد is a compound verbal expression ('came in/entered/upon') and it governs بخواب: here بخواب is not an imperative but denotes 'sleep' entering — i.e. 'sleep came upon (them)'. The clause therefore reads 'when Fortune awoke, sleep came upon (them)'.
segments.s235-g11: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'finally' or 'in the end'. زان is the old/poetic form of 'from that' ('of that'), so زان لشکر بیشمار = 'of that countless army/host'. The adjective بیشمار follows the noun as is common in Persian ('countless').
segments.s235-g12: سواری is the noun 'rider/horseman' and is the logical subject/thing being counted; نماند is the past negative of ماندن used here with the sense 'was left / remained' (so 'no rider remained'). از در کارزار is a locational phrase: از here functions as 'on'/'in' the sense 'in the area of', در is 'in' and کارزار 'battlefield/field of battle'. The overall order places the verb before the locative phrase in a somewhat elevated/poetic word order: 'no rider remained in the battlefield.'
segments.s235-g13: بزرگان means 'the great ones' (senior/important men). و is the coordinating 'and'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. نامور is an adjective 'renowned', and مهتران (a plural of مهتر) means 'chiefs/leaders' (here 'famed chiefs'). The phrase lists the actors: 'the great ones and those renowned chiefs'.
segments.s235-g14: ببستند is the past tense 'they bound / they tied' (perfect past of بستن). یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely / completely' modifying the action. ببند here is used as a verbal noun or describing the object 'bindings' (in poetry verbal forms are often nominalized), and گران is the adjective 'heavy' qualifying those bindings: 'they bound completely (with) heavy bindings'. The sequence is a poetic inversion but the meaning is 'they bound/fastened them entirely with heavy fastenings.'
segments.s235-g15: بخواری is an adverbial/nominal form meaning 'in humiliation' or 'with disgrace'. فگندند is the past tense 'they threw' (فگندند = they cast). بر is the preposition 'on/upon', پشت is 'back' and پیل is 'elephant' — together 'they cast (them) in humiliation upon the elephant's back.' Note the verb فگندند governs the following prepositional phrase indicating where the action occurred.
segments.s235-g16: سپه is 'the army/host' and is the subject of the copula بود ('was' in past). گرد آمده is a perfect participial compound meaning 'gathered around' or 'assembled about'. بر is the preposition 'around/on', and میل here is best read as 'pillar/mast' (classical use for a standard-pole); the phrase thus means the army was gathered about the two standards/poles (i.e. assembled around those landmarks). The word-order (copula + participle + prepositional phrase) is typical of elevated/epic diction.
segments.s235-g41: چه سازیم و این را چه درمان کنیم is two linked interrogative phrases. چه سازیم (چه + سازیم) uses چه as an interrogative/exclamative 'what/how' and سازیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive ('shall/we do' or 'what shall we make/arrange'), expressing deliberation. این را is the direct object with the enclitic object marker را; چه درمان کنیم repeats چه and uses درمان (a noun 'remedy/treatment') with کنیم (1pl subjunctive of کردن) to mean 'how shall we remedy/treat this.' The conjunction و simply links the two parallel questions.
segments.s235-g42: نشاید که این بر دل آسان کنیم is an impersonal negative judgement followed by a subordinate clause. نشاید is an impersonal/modal predicate 'it is not fitting/should not be.' که introduces the subordinate clause. این is the thing in question, بر دل is a prepositional phrase 'upon the heart' (دل = heart), and آسان کنیم (آسان + کنیم) uses آسان as adjective 'easy' with کنیم (1pl subjunctive of کردن) to mean 'to make easy' or 'to soothe.' The whole subordinate clause therefore means 'that we make this easy on the heart,' which نشاید negates: 'it is not fitting that we soothe the heart about this.'
segments.s235-g43: گر ایدونک رستم بود پیش رو is a conditional clause. گر is the classical conditional 'if.' ایدونک is a small adverbial/particle here meaning 'perhaps/by chance' (a poetic particle). رستم بود combines the proper name Rostam with بود (past/auxiliary 'was' used here in a counterfactual conditional: 'had Rostam been'). پیش رو is a compound adverbial 'in front' or 'before (him/there)' where پیش = 'before/ahead' and رو here contributes the sense 'facing/ahead.'
segments.s235-g44: نماند برین بوم و بر خار و خو is the apodosis (result) of the conditional. نماند is negative past/resultative 'would not remain/there would be none left.' برین is بر + این (here orthographic/poetic 'this'), so برین بوم = 'on this land.' The following coordinated phrase بر خار و خو repeats the preposition بر ('on') with خار 'thorns' and خو (a poetic word here meaning resting‑places/abodes or places of repose) to list parts of the land; the sense is emphatic total destruction 'not even on the soil, the thorns, or the resting-places would anything remain.'
segments.s235-g45: که من دستبرد ورا دیده‌ام is an explanatory subordinate clause introduced by که 'that/for.' من is the emphatic subject 'I.' دستبرد is a noun 'plundering/forcible seizure' and ورا is the attached third‑person object pronoun 'him/it' ('his plundering' or 'the plundering of him/against him'), placed before the verb in customary classical word order. دیده‌ام is the perfect past 'I have seen,' so the sequence is subject + object + verb (SOV) with 'که' linking it to what was said before.
segments.s235-g46: ز کار آگهان نیز بشنیده‌ام is a second, parallel statement of knowledge. ز here means 'from/about' (poetic preposition), کار is 'deed/affair,' آگهان in context functions as 'the knowing/informed ones' (reporting sources) and is the source of the report, نیز 'also' links this hearing to the prior seeing, and بشنیده‌ام is the perfect 'I have heard.' Grammatically this is a prepositional phrase (ز کار آگهان) + adverb (نیز) + verb (بشنیده‌ام).
segments.s235-g47: که او با بزرگان ایران‌زمین introduces the agent and company: که 'that' (linking/explanatory), او 'he,' با marks accompaniment 'with,' and بزرگان ایران‌زمین is the noun phrase 'the great/great‑men of the land of Iran' (بزرگان = nobles/elders; ایران‌زمین = Iran‑land). The whole phrase sets up who committed the subsequent action.
segments.s235-g48: چه کردست از نیکوی روز کین is the report of action. چه کردست is an exclamatory/retrospective 'what he has done' where کردست is an older contracted 3rd‑person perfect ('has done' / 'did'). از introduces the temporal/occasional phrase 'from/on' and نیکوی روز کین is a descriptive genitive: نیکوی (an adjective 'good' used here poetically/ironically) + روز 'day' + کین 'of vengeance' → 'on that dreadful/vengeful day.' Together the clause asks/declares what he did to the nobles on that day.
segments.s235-g65: The preposition ز (z) at the start marks the source: “from the nobles.” شاه (the king) is the grammatical subject and پاسخ (reply) is the object; شنید is the past tense verb (3rd sg.) “heard.” Word order (source — subject — object — verb) is a poetic inversion but yields “the king heard an answer from the nobles.”
segments.s235-g66: ز لشکر (from the army) again uses ز to mark source/selection. زبان‌آوری here functions as a noun meaning ‘one of eloquence’ or ‘a spokesman,’ the direct object of برگزید, the past (3rd sg.) verb “chose/selected.” The implied subject is the king carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s235-g67: دلیران و گردنکشان (the brave and the rebels/proud ones) is the compound direct object marked by را. بخواند is the past (3rd sg.) form of خواندن used here in the sense “called/summoned.” The subject is still understood (the king).
segments.s235-g68: This lists the things they abandoned: ز خواب و ز آرام و خوردن = “from sleep and from repose and from eating.” بماند is past (3rd sg.) meaning “remained/be left” (i.e. they were left without these things or gave them up). The repeated ز is a poetic preposition equivalent to modern از.
segments.s235-g69: در گنج بگشاد literally “he opened in the treasure” (i.e. opened the treasury); بگشاد is a poetic past form of گشودن “open.” و دینار داد follows with داد as past “gave,” so “he opened the treasure and gave dinars.”
segments.s235-g70: روان را marks the beneficiary (“to the souls/spirits”). بخون دل is a compressed poetic phrase meaning “with the blood of the heart” (ب = با ‘with’ + خون دل ‘heart’s blood’). آهار داد is a figurative expression: آهار (a fortifying/stiffening, here ‘rousing strength/composure’) + داد “gave” = “he gave a strengthening/rousing to their souls.”
segments.s235-g71: چنان شد is a stock result clause “so/thus it happened.” ز گردان جنگی زمین is a compressed phrase meaning roughly “the earth (زمین) from the warlike ranks (ز گردان جنگی)” — poetically indicating the earth was full of military arrays; گردان جنگی = “warlike troop/unit.”
segments.s235-g72: که introduces the ensuing image: گفتی is idiomatic here (“you would say” / “one might say”); سپهر اندر آمد بکین is a vivid simile — literally “the sky entered in vengeance” — where اندر آمد is “came in/entered” and بکین (poetic) conveys the notion of vengeful action or taking vengeance, producing “one would say the heavens had come in wrath/vengeance.”
segments.s235-g81: بدو is an older/directional clitic meaning 'to/for him' placed before the wished-for phrase. جان شاه جهان is a genitive chain 'the life/soul of the king of the world' (جان <- شاه <- جهان). شاد باد is an optative formula: شاد (happy) + باد (optative particle 'may it be'), so the whole segment reads 'May the king of the world's life be happy for him.'
segments.s235-g82: بر و بوم is an idiomatic pair meaning 'land and dwelling/home'. ایرانش is 'ایران' with the possessive -ش 'his/its' referring back to the king. آباد باد is again the optative 'may (it be) prosperous' (آباد = prosperous/inhabited, باد = 'may it be'). The order is noun phrase + optative verb phrase.
segments.s235-g83: همه is the distributive 'all' (here: 'all of us'). مر ترا is an archaic/clipped expression addressing the addressee ('to you' / 'for you'); it appears split in the listing (مر + ت + را) but functions as 'for you/us toward you' in this formula. چاکر و بنده‌ایم is the predication: two nominal predicates 'servant/retainer' and 'slave/humble one' joined by و and the copula -ایم ('we are'), so 'we are all your chākir and bende-īm (servants/slaves)'.
segments.s235-g84: بفرمان is a prepositional phrase 'by (your) order/at command'. رایت 'banner/standard' is coordinated with بفرمان by و so the phrase reads 'by order and (by) the standard'. سرافگنده‌ایم is a compound adjective 'سَر + افگنده' literally 'head-cast-down' with the first-person-plural copula -ایم: 'we are ashamed/abashed'. The clause states the group's shame under command and the banner.
segments.s235-g85: وزان = وَ + از آن: a connective 'and/from that' linking to what follows. جایگه means 'place' and is the locative source. شاد modifies لشکر ('the army, glad'), and براند is a form of راندن 'to drive/lead' used here as 'marched off/was driven forth' (the army moved). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'From that place [the] glad army marched off.'
segments.s235-g86: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. بسغد ('بِ سَغد') means 'to/in Sogd (Sogdiana)' and functions as a directional/locative complement. و links the two verbs; دو هفته is a temporal phrase 'two weeks'; بماند here is the past sense 'he remained/stayed' (ب- prefixed present form used in classical narrative as past). So: 'He came to Sogd and stayed two weeks.'
segments.s235-g87: بنخچیر means 'booty/spoils' (the object taken). گور و بمی here names places or sources (Gūr and Bam) in coordination with و — in older texts place-names can appear without added prepositions. دست برد is a verbal idiom 'to lay hands on / to take' (دست 'hand' + برد 'took/obtained'), so the whole segment says he took the spoils of Gūr and Bam.
segments.s235-g88: ازین گونه = 'from this sort/that kind' (از + این; here ین is the orthographic variant of این). یک چند is an idiom 'a few / some amount' and خورد here is the verb 'خوردن' 'to eat' (used literally: 'he ate some of it'); شمرد is 'he counted' (perfect/past narrative). So: 'From that lot he ate a little and counted [the rest].'
segments.s235-g25: گر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). آیند is a third-person plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they come' (classical narrative allows present-form for event after 'if'). زی is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (here 'upon us' or 'against us' in the sense 'coming at us'). The noun phrase ما (us) is the object of زی. برزم here stands in the sense of 'to battle / into battle' (archaic/poetic); آن گروه is the subject 'that band/troop' that would perform the coming. Word order is conditional + verb phrase + prepositional phrase + subject (typical for older Persian poetic diction).
segments.s235-g26: شود is a simple present/future-like copula 'become' (classical narrative use). کوه هامون places the predicate 'a mountain' before the proper name 'H̱amun' for emphasis: 'Hamun becomes a mountain.' The second half repeats the image (هامون چو کوه) with چو 'like/as' to make a simile. The repetition is a poetic intensification: first line states transformation, second likens the plain to a mountain.
segments.s235-g27: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when.' افراسیاب is the subject. این سخنها is the object phrase 'these words' (سخنها = plural). شنود is an archaic/poetic form 'he heard' (classical present/past usage fits narrative past). The clause is a straightforward temporal clause 'when Afrasiab heard these words.'
segments.s235-g28: دلش (دل + -ش) = 'his heart.' گشت is the past/resultative 'became/turned.' پر ز + noun is an idiom meaning 'full of ...' so پر درد = 'full of pain' (درد here = emotional sorrow). The second coordinate clause mirrors the first: سر پر ز دود = 'his head full of smoke.' The conjunction و links the two result clauses; overall this gives two parallel states resulting from hearing the words.
segments.s235-g29: همه 'all/every' modifies the coordinated nouns موبدان و ردان (mōbeds and rēdān). را is the definite object marker attaching to the coordinated noun phrase. بخواند is a classical/perfective verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (one of the listed senses: to call/summon). The verb is in the past narrative form used in epic style.
segments.s235-g30: ز is the poetic genitive/prepositional 'because of / concerning' or 'from.' کار گذشته literally 'the past affair(s)' or 'what had occurred before.' فراوان here qualifies the quantity/extent ('many' or 'at length'). براند is a classical third-person singular verb form from راندن, 'to send/drive (forth)'; in context it means 'he sent/drave them about/onto' those past matters. The phrase as a whole says he dispatched them concerning many past affairs.
segments.s235-g31: کز (here = 'from') marks origin: 'from Iran.' یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective 'one' or 'a certain.' لشکری is a collective noun 'army/host' and جنگجوی qualifies it as 'warlike' or 'of warriors.' The phrase says 'from Iran one warlike army (appeared).' Word order (origin + numeral + collective + adjective) reflects classical poetic compression.
segments.s235-g32: بدان here functions as the compound prepositional phrase 'به + آن' (to/for those), addressing the following noun; دان as listed in the lexicon does not function in its 'to know/consider' senses here (it participates in the compound). نامداران is the plural 'the renowned/celebrated ones.' نهادست is a perfective/resultative 'is placed/was set' (i.e., 'he set/entrusted'). روی literally 'face' is idiomatic for 'face/direction' — 'نهادست روی' means 'he set his face toward' or 'he fixed his purpose upon' those famed men. The clause therefore means he turned/committed (his intent or command) to those renowned men.
segments.s235-g57: ز رستم چرا بیم داری همی is a classical Persian word order where the preposition ز ('of/from') precedes the proper name رستم and marks the object/source of the fear: “fear of Rostam.” چرا is the interrogative “why.” داری is the present-tense verb 'to have' used here idiomatically as 'to feel/have fear' (you fear). همی is the archaic/poetic continuous/emphatic particle that follows the verb in classical Persian and gives an ongoing or emphatic shade (“you do fear / you are indeed fearing”).
segments.s235-g58: چنین کام دشمن بخاری همی: چنین + کام is a noun phrase 'such a desire' (چنین = 'thus/so', کام = 'desire/wish'), modified by دشمن 'the enemy' (dative/possessor relation: 'the enemy's desire'). همی again is the archaic emphatic/continuous particle. The item بخاری is archaic/ambiguous in this context (see senses); syntactically the clause says that by your action you are fulfilling or granting such an enemy-desire.
segments.s235-g59: ز مادر همه مرگ را زاده‌ایم: ز مادر is a prepositional phrase 'from the mother' (source). همه quantifies the subject 'all of us.' مرگ with the object marker را is marked as a definite/foregrounded object ('death' as the destined condition). زاده‌ایم is the classical perfect/passive-like first-person-plural 'we have been born' (i.e. 'we are born unto/for death').
segments.s235-g60: میان تا ببستیم نگشاده‌ایم: میان 'between' sets a temporal/spatial interval. تا here functions as 'until' (introducing the limit). ببستیم is first-person-plural classical form meaning 'we close/tie/bind' (subjunctive/imperative flavour in older Persian). نگشاده‌ایم is a negated perfect form from گشاد (ن + گشاده‌ایم) meaning 'we have not opened' or 'we have not unloosed.' Together the clause expresses that over that interval we have not undone/opened what was closed.
segments.s235-g61: اگر خاک ما را بپی بسپرند is a conditional protasis introduced by اگر 'if.' خاک ما را is the definite object 'our dust/earth' (را marks object). بپی is an old/contracted particle here functioning as an intensifier or adverbial augment ('completely/entirely' or similar nuance in classical usage). بسپرند is third-person-plural present/subjunctive of سپردن 'to consign/entrust' used here in the sense 'they bury/consign (our dust).' The whole clause: 'If they bury our bodies/our dust...'.
segments.s235-g62: ازین کرده خویش کیفر برند: ازین is a fused classical form meaning 'from this' (از + این). کرده خویش = 'their done deed' (کرده = 'what has been done', خویش = 'their own' / reflexive possession in context). کیفر برند literally 'they will take/carry punishment' — برند (برد) here functions as 'bear' or 'suffer' (third-person plural), so the clause says they will suffer punishment for this action.
segments.s235-g63: بکین‌گر ببندیم زین پس میان: بکین‌گر is an agentive/occupational noun (vocative or exhortative tone here: 'maker/one who does bekīn' or more generally 'binders'); ببندیم is first-person-plural subjunctive/imperative of بستن 'let us bind/close.' زین پس is a fixed classical phrase 'from now on' or 'henceforth' (زین = demonstrative/archaism + پس 'after'). میان here follows as a locative/adverbial 'in between' or 'among (them),' completing the expression of where/how to bind.
segments.s235-g64: نماند کسی زنده ز ایرانیان: نماند is a negative resultative 'there will remain / will be left (not)' in older Persian idiom (here translated 'will not remain'). کسی 'anyone' (indefinite). زنده 'alive.' ز ایرانیان 'of the Iranians' (ز = 'from/of' + ایرانیان = 'Iranians'). Together: 'there will remain no one alive from the Iranians.'
segments.s235-g17: ز + noun here gives the causal sense 'because of / from' (poetic use). کشته is used as a noun 'the slain'; چنان بد ... که is the standard 'so ... that' correlative: چنان (so) + بد (bad/terrible) + که introducing the result clause. در رزمگاه is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/on the battlefield') placed after که by poetic word-order.
segments.s235-g18: کسی را uses را as an object/affected‑person marker ('for anyone'); نبد is an archaic negative/past-of‑existence meaning 'there was not' (negated existence). جای + infinitive رفتن functions as 'a place to go/retreat to' and براه is an adverbial 'on the road / en route' — together: 'there was no place for anyone to go on the road.'
segments.s235-g19: وزین روی is an epithet 'with a dignified/grave face' (وزین = dignified; روی = face/appearance) modifying پیران 'the elders'. براه (poetic) means 'on the way/toward' and follows the noun; ختن here is likely a proper name/place (toward the Khitans) rather than the verb 'to circumcise', so it functions as a destination.
segments.s235-g20: بشد is narrative past/result 'it happened/they became' (here: 'an assembly was formed'). با marks accompaniment 'with', یکی نامدار is 'a certain famed one' (یکی = a certain), and انجمن is the noun 'assembly/meeting'. Word order is verb + prepositional accompaniment + noun phrase (poetic compactness).
segments.s235-g21: A triadic list of names (کشانی و شگنی و وهری) functions as the subject; these are obscure/tribal names here (treated as proper nouns). نماند is 'remained' (there remained), used with the following relative clause to mean 'there was no one left who ...'.
segments.s235-g22: که introduces a relative clause 'who ...'. منشور شمشیر رستم is a genitive chain 'the inscription/inscribed tablet of Rostam's sword' (منشور here = an inscribed tablet/inscription rather than 'manifesto'), and نخواند is the negative past infinitive/past form 'did not read/recite'. Combined with the previous clause, the construction yields 'there remained none who had not read...'.
segments.s235-g23: وزین روی again 'with that dignified countenance' modifies the action. تنگ here means 'close/tightly' and modifies اندر آمد (came in/closed in) — سپاه is the subject 'the army/host'. The verb phrase تنگ اندر آمد سپاه = 'the host came in closely / pressed in'.
segments.s235-g24: بپیش 'forward/before' + اندرون 'the inner quarters' + رستم names whose inner place this is; کینه‌خواه is an epithet in apposition 'the Avenger'. This nominal cluster links to the preceding verb (the host came forward before the inner court; Rostam the Avenger is presented by name/apposition).
segments.s235-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. این بند بد را 'this bad بند' (بند = stanza/episode/section) is the direct object marked by را. سر آمد is a light/verbal compound where سر (literally 'head/top') + آمد 'came' = 'came to an end' (i.e. 'ended'); کلید stands after that clause and will function as the subject of the following verb.
segments.s235-g74: فریبرز is a proper name (the subject). نزدیک here is an adverbial/adjective meaning 'near' (it modifies رسید). رستم is the place/person toward whom Fereyburz approaches. رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached'. Word order is subject — adverbial — indirect object — verb (typical Persian flexibility).
segments.s235-g75: بدل here means 'in return' or 'as a recompense' (an adverbial/coverb). شاد 'joyful' describes the giver's mood. با introduces what was given 'with/by (way of) the robe', and خلعت شهریار is the compound 'the king's robe of honour' (خلعت = robe of honour, شهریار = king). The sequence describes the gift given in recompense.
segments.s235-g76: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him' (i.e. he received it). اندرون here means 'inside' or 'inner' (often used of inner parts/quarters). تاج گوهر نگار is a nominal phrase: تاج 'crown', گوهر 'gem/jewel' qualifies the crown, and نگار is an epithet (here functioning to indicate ornamentation/beauty). Together: 'an inner crown adorned with jewels' given to him.
segments.s235-g77: ازان 'thereupon/from that' links back to the prior action. شادمان شد is a two-word predicate 'became joyful' (شادمان adjective + شد 'became'). گو here is a short poetic particle used before a name/phrase (roughly 'so'/'then' or marking the following name) and پیلتن is a place-name or proper name functioning as subject (so: 'Piltan became joyful').
segments.s235-g78: بزرگان لشکر is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the great men of the army' (بزرگان = 'great ones'; لشکر = 'army'). شدند is the past plural of شدن 'became' or here 'gathered/assembled' as a copular/auxiliary, and انجمن is the resulting noun 'assembly/meeting'. The phrase means 'the army's chiefs/great men became an assembly' = 'they formed an assembly.'
segments.s235-g79: گرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took/began' used idiomatically here with بر + noun to mean 'they uttered/poured (praise) upon'. بر پهلوان 'upon/for the champion' shows the target of their action (بر = toward/for). آفرین is a noun meaning 'acclamation' or the interjection 'bravo' — together: 'they shouted/bravoed for the champion.'
segments.s235-g80: که introduces a wish/clause here. آباد 'prosperous' is the predicate adjective. بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may it be' (classical optative). برستم is here formed from the verb برس (listed senses include the imperative/optative 'reach/come'); in this construction with بادا it functions as 'may ... come/arrive' or 'may ... be conferred'. زمین 'earth/land' is the scope/goal of the wish. Altogether: an optative 'May the earth be prosperous.'

sentences.s235-s25: What has he done to the king of Mazandaran, and what has his club brought upon those chiefs?
sentences.s235-s26: The illustrious men prepared a reply; all at once they rose from their places.
sentences.s235-s27: For if the famed men of S(ur) and China engaged in battle in Iran and sought vengeance,
sentences.s235-s28: Neither has anyone been lost from our army, nor has this country been made constantly drenched in blood.
sentences.s235-s17: That formidable host was broken; such the array — and that boundless army.
sentences.s235-s18: From sorrow, Kamus and the Khagan of China (were) —
sentences.s235-s19: Thus the troops were hemmed in and became weary; two divisions were secured from the rebels.
sentences.s235-s20: They drew (them) to Iran on the back of an elephant; the ground was full of blood for several miles.
sentences.s235-s1: Then a report reached Afrasiab that fire had arisen from the sea of water.
sentences.s235-s2: A new defeat befell the land of Turan at the hands of Kamus, Manšur, and the khaghan of China.
sentences.s235-s3: An army from Iran came to fight, and thereby the turning wheel's way became constricted.
sentences.s235-s4: The fighting continued unchanged for forty days; day and night the world was of one color.
sentences.s235-s5: The sun was not seen for the riders' dust; when Fortune woke, sleep came upon them.
sentences.s235-s6: At last, of that countless host not a single rider remained on the field of battle.
sentences.s235-s7: The great ones and those famed chiefs bound them completely with heavy fastenings.
sentences.s235-s8: They threw them down in humiliation upon the elephant's back; the army stood gathered around (the two standards/poles).
sentences.s235-s21: What can we do, and how can we remedy this? It is not fitting to make this easy on the heart.
sentences.s235-s22: If, by any chance, Rostam had been in front, nothing would have remained on this land—neither on its soil nor on its thorns and resting-places.
sentences.s235-s23: For I have seen his plundering myself, and I have also heard of it from those who know.
sentences.s235-s24: That he, together with the great men of Iran, did what he did on that terrible day of vengeance.
sentences.s235-s33: The king heard an answer from the nobles and chose an orator from the army.
sentences.s235-s34: He summoned the brave and the rebels; they gave up sleep, rest, and food.
sentences.s235-s35: He opened the treasure and gave dinars; he bestowed a heart‑rousing strengthening on their souls with the heart’s blood.
sentences.s235-s36: Thus it came to pass from the warlike ranks that one would say the sky itself came in vengeance.
sentences.s235-s41: May the king of the world enjoy a happy life, and may the land and home of his Iran be prosperous.
sentences.s235-s42: We are all your servants and slaves; by command and beneath the standard we are ashamed.
sentences.s235-s43: From that place the joyful army set out; he came to Sogd and stayed two weeks.
sentences.s235-s44: He seized spoils from Gūr and Bam; from that sort he ate a little and counted [them].
sentences.s235-s13: If that band come upon us to fight, Hamun will become a mountain, and Hamun will be like a mountain.
sentences.s235-s14: When Afrasiab heard these words, his heart became full of pain and his head full of smoke.
sentences.s235-s15: He summoned all the mobeds and the rēdān, and sent them forth on many past affairs.
sentences.s235-s16: From Iran came one warlike host; he set his face upon those renowned men.
sentences.s235-s29: Why do you fear Rostam? In doing so you gratify the enemy's desire.
sentences.s235-s30: We are all born of the mother Death; meanwhile, between (birth and death) we have not unfastened (what was fastened).
sentences.s235-s31: If they consign our dust to the earth, they will bear punishment for this deed of theirs.
sentences.s235-s32: Let us bind (them) with bekins; henceforth, no one of the Iranians will remain alive.
sentences.s235-s9: So terrible was the slaughter that on the battlefield there was no place for anyone to go.
sentences.s235-s10: And with that grave bearing the elders took the road to the Khitans, and an assembly was formed with one famed man.
sentences.s235-s11: There remained none of the Kashani, Shagani, or Vahri who had not read the inscription on Rostam's sword.
sentences.s235-s12: With that solemn mien the host drew in close; before the inner court appeared Rostam the Avenger.
sentences.s235-s37: When this bad episode ended, the key came near Rostam.
sentences.s235-s38: As a reward, the joyful one (or the king) presented him with the king's robe and an inner crown set with gems.
sentences.s235-s39: Thereupon Piltan became joyful; the great men of the army assembled.
sentences.s235-s40: They raised cries of 'bravo' for the champion: 'May the earth be prosperous.'
